preFix.title: Rabbit colony system versus cage system
preFix.subtitle: The management and welfare trade-offs between the two main housing approaches.
preFix.excerpt: Colony systems let rabbits express natural social behaviour and are easier to manage at small scale. Cage systems allow individual monitoring, reduce disease transmission, and suit intensive production. Neither is universally better; the right choice depends on the scale, the keeper's time, and the production aim.
preFix.sourceNotes: Rabbit Welfare Association and Fund, housing guidance. Cornell Small Farms, Rabbit Production for Small Farms bulletin. British Rabbit Council housing standards.

content.text: depends on flock size and how closely you want to monitor each animal.

A colony allows rabbits to express social and exploratory behaviour that a cage system does not. Grow-out kits (post-weaning rabbits kept until slaughter weight) do better in colonies than in cages: they eat more, grow faster, and show fewer stereotypic behaviours. Management is simpler: fill the colony, clean it weekly, check animals daily, remove at slaughter weight. No individual cage management is required.

Disease spreads faster in a colony. A rabbit with snuffles or coccidiosis will infect colony-mates before the illness is noticed. Does must be separated to individual quarters before kindling; mixing a doe with kits in an open colony results in other does stealing and sometimes killing kits. Breeding management in a colony requires moving does in and out, which is more labour than managing cages individually.

Individual cages allow exact monitoring of feed intake, earlier detection of health problems, and precise control of breeding dates and litter sizes. Disease is contained to one animal when caught early. Cage systems are standard for commercial production because individual monitoring is worth the extra labour at scale.

For most small-scale producers, a hybrid works well. Does breed, kindle, and rear in individual cages; grow-out kits move to a colony pen from weaning to slaughter weight. This keeps control where it matters while giving the welfare benefits of colony housing during grow-out.
